revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:سونو جوي]][[bg:Сонно Джои]][[ca:Sonnō jōi]][[sn:commons:Category:Sonnō jōi]][[cs:Sonnó džói]][[de:Sonnō jōi]][[en:Sonnō jōi]][[es:Sonnō jōi]][[eu:Sonnō jōi]][[fa:سوننو جوئی]][[fi:Sonnō jōi]][[fr:Sonnō jōi]][[he:סונו ג'וי]][[hu:Szonnó dzsói]][[id:Sonnō jōi]][[it:Sonnō jōi]][[ja:尊王攘夷]][[ko:존왕양이]][[ms:Sonno joi]][[pt:Sonnō jōi]][[ru:Сонно Дзёи]][[sr:Соно џои]][[th:ซนโนโจอิ]][[tr:Sonnō jōi]][[uk:Сонно дзьої]][[vi:Tôn hoàng nhương di]][[zh-classical:尊王攘夷]][[zh-yue:尊王攘夷]][[zh:尊王攘夷]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1065356]] Wikidata name: Sonnō jōi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Nationalist slogan in 1850s Japan}}{{refimprove|date=September 2013}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Yokohama-Sumo-Wrestler-Defeating-a-Foreigner-1861-Ipposai-Yoshifuji.png|thumb|An 1861 image expressing the ''Jōi'' ({{lang|ja|攘夷}}, "Expel the Barbarians") sentiment]] {{nihongo|2=[[:wikt:尊|尊]][[:wikt:王|王]][[:wikt:攘|攘]][[:wikt:夷|夷]]|3='''Sonnō jōi'''|4="revere the Emperor, expel the barbarians"}} was a ''[[:en:Yojijukugo|Yojijukugo]]'' (four-character compound) phrase used as the rallying cry and [[:en:slogan|slogan]] of a [[:en:political movement|political movement]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] in the 1850s and 1860s, during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period. Based on [[:en:Neo-Confucianism|Neo-Confucianism]] and [[:en:Kokugaku|Japanese nativism]], the movement sought to overthrow the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and restore the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. {{Infobox Chinese | t = 尊王攘夷 | kanji = 尊王攘夷 | kana = そんのうじょうい | romaji = Sonnō jōi | p = Zunwang Rangyi | j = zyun1 wong4 joeng4 ji4 }}
== Etymology == ''Sonnō jōi'' is the [[:en:On'yomi|Japanese reading]] of the Chinese idiom ''Zunwang Rangyi'' ({{lang|zh-Hant|尊王攘夷}}; {{lit}} "Revere the King, Expel the Barbarians"). During the [[:en:Spring and Autumn period|Spring and Autumn period]] of China, Chancellor [[:en:Guan Zhong|Guan Zhong]] of [[:en:Qi (state)|Qi]] initiated a policy known as ''Zunwang Rangyi'', in reference to the [[:en:Zhou kings|Zhou kings]].<ref name=po5-65>{{cite book | last=Poo | first=Mu-chou | title=Enemies of Civilization | year=2005 | publisher=State University of New York Press | location=Albany | isbn=0-7914-6364-8 | page=65}}</ref> Adopting and adhering to it, [[:en:Duke Huan of Qi|Duke Huan of Qi]] assembled the Chinese feudal lords to strike down the threat of barbarians from China.<ref>{{cite book | last=Tillman | first=Hoyt Cleveland | title=Utilitarian Confucianism: Ch'en Liang's Challenge to Chu Hsi | year=1982 | publisher=Harvard University Press | location=Cambridge | isbn=0-674-93176-9 | page=64}}</ref> For it, [[:en:Confucius|Confucius]] himself praised Guan Zhong for the preservation of Chinese civilization through the example of the [[:en:Hua-Yi distinction|contrast]] in the hairstyles and [[:en:Hanfu|clothing styles]] between them and barbaric peoples.<ref name=po5-65/> Through the ''[[:en:Analects|Analects]]'' of Confucius, the Chinese expression came to be transmitted to Japan as ''sonnō jōi''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Holcombe | first=Charles | title=A History of East Asia: From the Origins of Civilization to the Twenty-first Century | year=2010 | publisher=Cambridge University Press | location=Cambridge | isbn=978-0-521-73164-5 | page=216}}</ref>
== Philosophy == The origin of the philosophy as used in Japan can be traced to the Confucian classic the Gongyang Commentary of the ''Chunqiu''. The Tokugawa shogunate promulgated the Zhu Xi school of Neo-Confucianism (''Shushi-gaku''), which interpreted the ''Chunqiu'' using this concept. 17th-century Confucian scholars [[:en:Yamazaki Ansai|Yamazaki Ansai]] and [[:en:Yamaga Sokō|Yamaga Sokō]] wrote on the sanctity of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] and its superiority to the ruling houses of other nations. These ideas were expanded by [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] scholar [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], and seen in Takenouchi Shikibu's theory of absolute loyalty to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] ([[:ja:尊皇論|尊皇論]] ''sonnōron''), that implied that less loyalty should be given to the ruling Tokugawa shogunate.
[[:en:Mitogaku|Mitogaku]] scholar [[:en:Aizawa Seishisai|Aizawa Seishisai]] introduced the term ''sonnō jōi'' into modern Japanese in his work ''Shinron'' in 1825, where ''sonnō'' was regarded as the reverence expressed by the Tokugawa Shogunate to the emperor and ''jōi'' was the [[History of Roman Catholicism in Japan#Persecution and martyrdom|proscription of Christianity]].
''Sonnō jōi'' was an [[:en:anti-imperialist|anti-imperialist]] expression of [[:en:Western imperialism|Western imperialism]] before Japan transitioned to imperialism.<ref>{{cite book |editor=Viren Murthy |title=Pan-Asianism and the Legacy of the Chinese Revolution |page=25 |date=2023 |publisher=University of Chicago Press}}</ref>
==Influence== [[File:Mito rebellion samurai under Sonno Joi banner.jpg|thumb|Part of a woodblock print by [[:ja:歌川国輝 (3代目)|Utagawa Kuniteru III]] depicting [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] under a ''sonnō jōi'' banner during the 1864 [[:en:Mito Rebellion|Mito Rebellion]] ]] With the increasing number of incursions of foreign ships into Japanese waters in the late 18th and early 19th century, the ''[[:en:Sakoku|Sakoku]]'' ("national seclusion") policy came increasingly into question. The ''jōi'' "expel the barbarians" portion of ''sonnō jōi'', changed into a reaction against the [[:en:Convention of Kanagawa|Convention of Kanagawa]] of 1854, which opened Japan to foreign trade. Under military threat from [[:en:United States Navy|United States Navy]] [[Commodore (Rank)|Commodore]] [[:en:Matthew C. Perry|Matthew C. Perry]]'s so-called "[[:en:Black Ships|black ships]]", the treaty was signed under duress and was vehemently opposed in ''[[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]'' quarters. The fact that the Tokugawa Shogunate was powerless against the foreigners despite the will expressed by the Imperial court was taken as evidence by [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] and other anti-Tokugawa leaders that the ''sonnō'' (revere the Emperor) portion of the philosophy was not working, and that the Shogunate must be replaced by a government more able to show its loyalty to the Emperor by enforcing the Emperor’s will.
The philosophy was thus adopted as a battle cry of the rebellious regions of [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū Domain]] and [[:en:Satsuma Province|Satsuma Province]]. The Imperial court in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] sympathized with the movement. [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Emperor Kōmei]] personally agreed with such sentiments, and – breaking with centuries of imperial tradition – personally began to take an active role in matters of state: as opportunities arose, he fulminated against the treaties and attempted to interfere in the shogunal succession. His efforts culminated in March 1863 with his "[[:en:Order to Expel Barbarians|Order to Expel Barbarians]]" ({{lang|ja|攘夷勅命}}). Although the Shogunate had no intention of enforcing the order, it nevertheless inspired attacks against the Shogunate itself and against foreigners in Japan, the most notable incident being the killing of the trader [[:en:Charles Lennox Richardson|Charles Lennox Richardson]]. Other attacks included the shelling of foreign shipping in [[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki]].<ref>Hagiwara, p. 35.</ref> ''[[:en:Rōnin|Rōnin]]s'' (masterless samurai) also rallied to the cause, assassinating Shogunate officials and Westerners.
This turned out to be the zenith of the ''sonnō jōi'' movement, since the [[:en:Western world|Western powers]] responded by demanding [[:en:Reparations (transitional justice)|reparations]] for the assassinations and other acts by samurai against Western interests. In 1864, four Western nations launched a campaign against Shimonoseki, overrunning the meager defences and briefly occupying the region. While this incident showed that Japan was no match for Western military powers, it also served to further weaken the Shogunate, permitting the rebel provinces to ally and overthrow it, bringing about the [[Meiji Restoration]].
The slogan itself was never actually a government or rebel policy; for all its rhetoric, Satsuma in particular had close ties with the West, purchasing guns, artillery, ships and other technology.
==Legacy== After the symbolic restoration of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]], the ''sonnō jōi'' slogan was replaced with ''[[:en:Fukoku kyōhei|Fukoku kyōhei]]'' ([[:ja:富国強兵|富国強兵]]), or "enrich the nation, strengthen the armies", the rallying call of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and the seed of its actions during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].{{fact|date=November 2023}}
==See also== *This phrase is featured and examined in [[:en:James Clavell|James Clavell]]'s ''[[:en:Gai-Jin (novel)|Gai-Jin: A Novel of Japan]]'' *[[:en:Kinmon incident|Kinmon incident]] *''[[:en:Shishi (organization)|Shishi]]'' *[[:en:Shōwa Restoration|Shōwa Restoration]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Akamatsu, Paul. (1972). ''Meiji 1868: Revolution and Counter-Revolution in Japan'' (Miriam Kochan, translator). New York: Harper & Row. * [[:en:William G. Beasley|Beasley, William G.]] (1972). ''The Meiji Restoration.'' Stanford: Stanford University Press. * [[:en:Albert M. Craig|Craig, Albert M.]] (1961). ''Chōshū in the Meiji Restoration.'' Cambridge: Harvard University Press. * [[:en:Marius Jansen|Jansen, Marius B.]] and [[:en:Gilbert Rozman|Gilbert Rozman]], eds. (1986). ''Japan in Transition: from Tokugawa to Meiji.'' Princeton: [[:en:Princeton University Press|Princeton University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780691054599}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/12311985 OCLC 12311985] * Jansen, Marius B. (2000). ''The Making of Modern Japan.'' Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780674003347}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/44090600 OCLC 44090600] *Shiba, Ryotaro. (1998). ''The Last Shogun: The Life of Tokugawa Yoshinobu''. Tokyo: Kodansha. {{ISBN|1-56836-246-3}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:سونو جوي]][[bg:Сонно Джои]][[ca:Sonnō jōi]][[commons:Category:Sonnō jōi]][[cs:Sonnó džói]][[de:Sonnō jōi]][[en:Sonnō jōi]][[es:Sonnō jōi]][[eu:Sonnō jōi]][[fa:سوننو جوئی]][[fi:Sonnō jōi]][[fr:Sonnō jōi]][[he:סונו ג'וי]][[hu:Szonnó dzsói]][[id:Sonnō jōi]][[it:Sonnō jōi]][[ja:尊王攘夷]][[ko:존왕양이]][[ms:Sonno joi]][[pt:Sonnō jōi]][[ru:Сонно Дзёи]][[sr:Соно џои]][[th:ซนโนโจอิ]][[tr:Sonnō jōi]][[uk:Сонно дзьої]][[vi:Tôn hoàng nhương di]][[zh_classical:尊王攘夷]][[zh_yue:尊王攘夷]][[zh:尊王攘夷]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:سونو جوي]][[bg:Сонно Джои]][[ca:Sonnō jōi]][[sn:commons:Category:Sonnō jōi]][[cs:Sonnó džói]][[de:Sonnō jōi]][[en:Sonnō jōi]][[es:Sonnō jōi]][[eu:Sonnō jōi]][[fa:سوننو جوئی]][[fi:Sonnō jōi]][[fr:Sonnō jōi]][[he:סונו ג'וי]][[hu:Szonnó dzsói]][[id:Sonnō jōi]][[it:Sonnō jōi]][[ja:尊王攘夷]][[ko:존왕양이]][[ms:Sonno joi]][[pt:Sonnō jōi]][[ru:Сонно Дзёи]][[sr:Соно џои]][[th:ซนโนโจอิ]][[tr:Sonnō jōi]][[uk:Сонно дзьої]][[vi:Tôn hoàng nhương di]][[zh-classical:尊王攘夷]][[zh-yue:尊王攘夷]][[zh:尊王攘夷]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1065356]] Wikidata name: Sonnō jōi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Nationalist slogan in 1850s Japan}}{{refimprove|date=September 2013}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Yokohama-Sumo-Wrestler-Defeating-a-Foreigner-1861-Ipposai-Yoshifuji.png|thumb|An 1861 image expressing the ''Jōi'' ({{lang|ja|攘夷}}, "Expel the Barbarians") sentiment]] {{nihongo|2=[[:wikt:尊|尊]][[:wikt:王|王]][[:wikt:攘|攘]][[:wikt:夷|夷]]|3='''Sonnō jōi'''|4="revere the Emperor, expel the barbarians"}} was a ''[[:en:Yojijukugo|Yojijukugo]]'' (four-character compound) phrase used as the rallying cry and [[:en:slogan|slogan]] of a [[:en:political movement|political movement]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] in the 1850s and 1860s, during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period. Based on [[:en:Neo-Confucianism|Neo-Confucianism]] and [[:en:Kokugaku|Japanese nativism]], the movement sought to overthrow the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and restore the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. {{Infobox Chinese | t = 尊王攘夷 | kanji = 尊王攘夷 | kana = そんのうじょうい | romaji = Sonnō jōi | p = Zunwang Rangyi | j = zyun1 wong4 joeng4 ji4 }}
== Etymology == ''Sonnō jōi'' is the [[:en:On'yomi|Japanese reading]] of the Chinese idiom ''Zunwang Rangyi'' ({{lang|zh-Hant|尊王攘夷}}; {{lit}} "Revere the King, Expel the Barbarians"). During the [[:en:Spring and Autumn period|Spring and Autumn period]] of China, Chancellor [[:en:Guan Zhong|Guan Zhong]] of [[:en:Qi (state)|Qi]] initiated a policy known as ''Zunwang Rangyi'', in reference to the [[:en:Zhou kings|Zhou kings]].<ref name=po5-65>{{cite book | last=Poo | first=Mu-chou | title=Enemies of Civilization | year=2005 | publisher=State University of New York Press | location=Albany | isbn=0-7914-6364-8 | page=65}}</ref> Adopting and adhering to it, [[:en:Duke Huan of Qi|Duke Huan of Qi]] assembled the Chinese feudal lords to strike down the threat of barbarians from China.<ref>{{cite book | last=Tillman | first=Hoyt Cleveland | title=Utilitarian Confucianism: Ch'en Liang's Challenge to Chu Hsi | year=1982 | publisher=Harvard University Press | location=Cambridge | isbn=0-674-93176-9 | page=64}}</ref> For it, [[:en:Confucius|Confucius]] himself praised Guan Zhong for the preservation of Chinese civilization through the example of the [[:en:Hua-Yi distinction|contrast]] in the hairstyles and [[:en:Hanfu|clothing styles]] between them and barbaric peoples.<ref name=po5-65/> Through the ''[[:en:Analects|Analects]]'' of Confucius, the Chinese expression came to be transmitted to Japan as ''sonnō jōi''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Holcombe | first=Charles | title=A History of East Asia: From the Origins of Civilization to the Twenty-first Century | year=2010 | publisher=Cambridge University Press | location=Cambridge | isbn=978-0-521-73164-5 | page=216}}</ref>
== Philosophy == The origin of the philosophy as used in Japan can be traced to the Confucian classic the Gongyang Commentary of the ''Chunqiu''. The Tokugawa shogunate promulgated the Zhu Xi school of Neo-Confucianism (''Shushi-gaku''), which interpreted the ''Chunqiu'' using this concept. 17th-century Confucian scholars [[:en:Yamazaki Ansai|Yamazaki Ansai]] and [[:en:Yamaga Sokō|Yamaga Sokō]] wrote on the sanctity of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] and its superiority to the ruling houses of other nations. These ideas were expanded by [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] scholar [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], and seen in Takenouchi Shikibu's theory of absolute loyalty to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] ([[:ja:尊皇論|尊皇論]] ''sonnōron''), that implied that less loyalty should be given to the ruling Tokugawa shogunate.
[[:en:Mitogaku|Mitogaku]] scholar [[:en:Aizawa Seishisai|Aizawa Seishisai]] introduced the term ''sonnō jōi'' into modern Japanese in his work ''Shinron'' in 1825, where ''sonnō'' was regarded as the reverence expressed by the Tokugawa Shogunate to the emperor and ''jōi'' was the [[History of Roman Catholicism in Japan#Persecution and martyrdom|proscription of Christianity]].
''Sonnō jōi'' was an [[:en:anti-imperialist|anti-imperialist]] expression of [[:en:Western imperialism|Western imperialism]] before Japan transitioned to imperialism.<ref>{{cite book |editor=Viren Murthy |title=Pan-Asianism and the Legacy of the Chinese Revolution |page=25 |date=2023 |publisher=University of Chicago Press}}</ref>
==Influence== [[File:Mito rebellion samurai under Sonno Joi banner.jpg|thumb|Part of a woodblock print by [[:ja:歌川国輝 (3代目)|Utagawa Kuniteru III]] depicting [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] under a ''sonnō jōi'' banner during the 1864 [[:en:Mito Rebellion|Mito Rebellion]] ]] With the increasing number of incursions of foreign ships into Japanese waters in the late 18th and early 19th century, the ''[[:en:Sakoku|Sakoku]]'' ("national seclusion") policy came increasingly into question. The ''jōi'' "expel the barbarians" portion of ''sonnō jōi'', changed into a reaction against the [[:en:Convention of Kanagawa|Convention of Kanagawa]] of 1854, which opened Japan to foreign trade. Under military threat from [[:en:United States Navy|United States Navy]] [[Commodore (Rank)|Commodore]] [[:en:Matthew C. Perry|Matthew C. Perry]]'s so-called "[[:en:Black Ships|black ships]]", the treaty was signed under duress and was vehemently opposed in ''[[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]'' quarters. The fact that the Tokugawa Shogunate was powerless against the foreigners despite the will expressed by the Imperial court was taken as evidence by [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] and other anti-Tokugawa leaders that the ''sonnō'' (revere the Emperor) portion of the philosophy was not working, and that the Shogunate must be replaced by a government more able to show its loyalty to the Emperor by enforcing the Emperor’s will.
The philosophy was thus adopted as a battle cry of the rebellious regions of [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū Domain]] and [[:en:Satsuma Province|Satsuma Province]]. The Imperial court in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] sympathized with the movement. [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Emperor Kōmei]] personally agreed with such sentiments, and – breaking with centuries of imperial tradition – personally began to take an active role in matters of state: as opportunities arose, he fulminated against the treaties and attempted to interfere in the shogunal succession. His efforts culminated in March 1863 with his "[[:en:Order to Expel Barbarians|Order to Expel Barbarians]]" ({{lang|ja|攘夷勅命}}). Although the Shogunate had no intention of enforcing the order, it nevertheless inspired attacks against the Shogunate itself and against foreigners in Japan, the most notable incident being the killing of the trader [[:en:Charles Lennox Richardson|Charles Lennox Richardson]]. Other attacks included the shelling of foreign shipping in [[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki]].<ref>Hagiwara, p. 35.</ref> ''[[:en:Rōnin|Rōnin]]s'' (masterless samurai) also rallied to the cause, assassinating Shogunate officials and Westerners.
This turned out to be the zenith of the ''sonnō jōi'' movement, since the [[:en:Western world|Western powers]] responded by demanding [[:en:Reparations (transitional justice)|reparations]] for the assassinations and other acts by samurai against Western interests. In 1864, four Western nations launched a campaign against Shimonoseki, overrunning the meager defences and briefly occupying the region. While this incident showed that Japan was no match for Western military powers, it also served to further weaken the Shogunate, permitting the rebel provinces to ally and overthrow it, bringing about the [[Meiji Restoration]].
The slogan itself was never actually a government or rebel policy; for all its rhetoric, Satsuma in particular had close ties with the West, purchasing guns, artillery, ships and other technology.
==Legacy== After the symbolic restoration of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]], the ''sonnō jōi'' slogan was replaced with ''[[:en:Fukoku kyōhei|Fukoku kyōhei]]'' ([[:ja:富国強兵|富国強兵]]), or "enrich the nation, strengthen the armies", the rallying call of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and the seed of its actions during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].{{fact|date=November 2023}}
==See also== *This phrase is featured and examined in [[:en:James Clavell|James Clavell]]'s ''[[:en:Gai-Jin (novel)|Gai-Jin: A Novel of Japan]]'' *[[:en:Kinmon incident|Kinmon incident]] *''[[:en:Shishi (organization)|Shishi]]'' *[[:en:Shōwa Restoration|Shōwa Restoration]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Akamatsu, Paul. (1972). ''Meiji 1868: Revolution and Counter-Revolution in Japan'' (Miriam Kochan, translator). New York: Harper & Row. * [[:en:William G. Beasley|Beasley, William G.]] (1972). ''The Meiji Restoration.'' Stanford: Stanford University Press. * [[:en:Albert M. Craig|Craig, Albert M.]] (1961). ''Chōshū in the Meiji Restoration.'' Cambridge: Harvard University Press. * [[:en:Marius Jansen|Jansen, Marius B.]] and [[:en:Gilbert Rozman|Gilbert Rozman]], eds. (1986). ''Japan in Transition: from Tokugawa to Meiji.'' Princeton: [[:en:Princeton University Press|Princeton University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780691054599}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/12311985 OCLC 12311985] * Jansen, Marius B. (2000). ''The Making of Modern Japan.'' Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780674003347}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/44090600 OCLC 44090600] *Shiba, Ryotaro. (1998). ''The Last Shogun: The Life of Tokugawa Yoshinobu''. Tokyo: Kodansha. {{ISBN|1-56836-246-3}}

Perry]]'s so-called "[[:en:Black Ships|black ships]]", the treaty was signed under duress and was vehemently opposed in ''[[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]'' quarters. Although the Shogunate had no intention of enforcing the order, it nevertheless inspired attacks against the Shogunate itself and against foreigners in Japan, the most notable incident being the killing of the trader [[:en:Charles Lennox Richardson|Charles Lennox Richardson]]. Other attacks included the shelling of foreign shipping in [[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki]].<ref>Hagiwara, p. 35.</ref> ''[[:en:Rōnin|Rōnin]]s'' (masterless samurai) also rallied to the cause, assassinating Shogunate officials and Westerners.
This turned out to be the zenith of the ''sonnō jōi'' movement, since the [[:en:Western world|Western powers]] responded by demanding [[:en:Reparations (transitional justice)|reparations]] for the assassinations and other acts by samurai against Western interests. In 1864, four Western nations launched a campaign against Shimonoseki, overrunning the meager defences and briefly occupying the region. While this incident showed that Japan was no match for Western military powers, it also served to further weaken the Shogunate, permitting the rebel provinces to ally and overthrow it, bringing about the [[Meiji Restoration]].
The slogan itself was never actually a government or rebel policy; for all its rhetoric, Satsuma in particular had close ties with the West, purchasing guns, artillery, ships and other technology.
==Legacy== After the symbolic restoration of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]], the ''sonnō jōi'' slogan was replaced with ''[[:en:Fukoku kyōhei|Fukoku kyōhei]]'' ([[:ja:富国強兵|富国強兵]]), or "enrich the nation, strengthen the armies", the rallying call of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and the seed of its actions during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].{{fact|date=November 2023}}
==See also== *This phrase is featured and examined in [[:en:James Clavell|James Clavell]]'s ''[[:en:Gai-Jin (novel)|Gai-Jin: A Novel of Japan]]'' *[[:en:Kinmon incident|Kinmon incident]] *''[[:en:Shishi (organization)|Shishi]]'' *[[:en:Shōwa Restoration|Shōwa Restoration]]

di]][[zh-classical:尊王攘夷]][[zh-yue:尊王攘夷]][[zh:尊王攘夷]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1065356]] Wikidata name: Sonnō jōi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Nationalist slogan in 1850s Japan}}{{refimprove|date=September 2013}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Yokohama-Sumo-Wrestler-Defeating-a-Foreigner-1861-Ipposai-Yoshifuji.png|thumb|An 1861 image expressing the ''Jōi'' ({{lang|ja|攘夷}}, "Expel the Barbarians") sentiment]] {{nihongo|2=[[:wikt:尊|尊]][[:wikt:王|王]][[:wikt:攘|攘]][[:wikt:夷|夷]]|3='''Sonnō jōi'''|4="revere the Emperor, expel the barbarians"}} was a ''[[:en:Yojijukugo|Yojijukugo]]'' (four-character compound) phrase used as the rallying cry and [[:en:slogan|slogan]] of a [[:en:political movement|political movement]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] in the 1850s and 1860s, during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period. Based on [[:en:Neo-Confucianism|Neo-Confucianism]] and [[:en:Kokugaku|Japanese nativism]], the movement sought to overthrow the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and restore the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. {{Infobox Chinese | t = 尊王攘夷 | kanji = 尊王攘夷 | kana = そんのうじょうい | romaji = Sonnō jōi | p = Zunwang Rangyi | j = zyun1 wong4 joeng4 ji4 }}
== Etymology == ''Sonnō jōi'' is the [[:en:On'yomi|Japanese reading]] of the Chinese idiom ''Zunwang Rangyi'' ({{lang|zh-Hant|尊王攘夷}}; {{lit}} "Revere the King, Expel the Barbarians"). During the [[:en:Spring and Autumn period|Spring and Autumn period]] of China, Chancellor [[:en:Guan Zhong|Guan Zhong]] of [[:en:Qi (state)|Qi]] initiated a policy known as ''Zunwang Rangyi'', in reference to the [[:en:Zhou kings|Zhou kings]].<ref name=po5-65>{{cite book | last=Poo | first=Mu-chou | title=Enemies of Civilization | year=2005 | publisher=State University of New York Press | location=Albany | isbn=0-7914-6364-8 | page=65}}</ref> Adopting and adhering to it, [[:en:Duke Huan of Qi|Duke Huan of Qi]] assembled the Chinese feudal lords to strike down the threat of barbarians from China.<ref>{{cite book | last=Tillman | first=Hoyt Cleveland | title=Utilitarian Confucianism: Ch'en Liang's Challenge to Chu Hsi | year=1982 | publisher=Harvard University Press | location=Cambridge | isbn=0-674-93176-9 | page=64}}</ref> For it, [[:en:Confucius|Confucius]] himself praised Guan Zhong for the preservation of Chinese civilization through the example of the [[:en:Hua-Yi distinction|contrast]] in the hairstyles and [[:en:Hanfu|clothing styles]] between them and barbaric peoples.<ref name=po5-65/> Through the ''[[:en:Analects|Analects]]'' of Confucius, the Chinese expression came to be transmitted to Japan as ''sonnō jōi''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Holcombe | first=Charles | title=A History of East Asia: From the Origins of Civilization to the Twenty-first Century | year=2010 | publisher=Cambridge University Press | location=Cambridge | isbn=978-0-521-73164-5 | page=216}}</ref>
== Philosophy == The origin of the philosophy as used in Japan can be traced to the Confucian classic the Gongyang Commentary of the ''Chunqiu''. The Tokugawa shogunate promulgated the Zhu Xi school of Neo-Confucianism (''Shushi-gaku''), which interpreted the ''Chunqiu'' using this concept. 17th-century Confucian scholars [[:en:Yamazaki Ansai|Yamazaki Ansai]] and [[:en:Yamaga Sokō|Yamaga Sokō]] wrote on the sanctity of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] and its superiority to the ruling houses of other nations. These ideas were expanded by [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] scholar [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], and seen in Takenouchi Shikibu's theory of absolute loyalty to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] ([[:ja:尊皇論|尊皇論]] ''sonnōron''), that implied that less loyalty should be given to the ruling Tokugawa shogunate.
[[:en:Mitogaku|Mitogaku]] scholar [[:en:Aizawa Seishisai|Aizawa Seishisai]] introduced the term ''sonnō jōi'' into modern Japanese in his work ''Shinron'' in 1825, where ''sonnō'' was regarded as the reverence expressed by the Tokugawa Shogunate to the emperor and ''jōi'' was the [[History of Roman Catholicism in Japan#Persecution and martyrdom|proscription of Christianity]].
''Sonnō jōi'' was an [[:en:anti-imperialist|anti-imperialist]] expression of [[:en:Western imperialism|Western imperialism]] before Japan transitioned to imperialism.<ref>{{cite book |editor=Viren Murthy |title=Pan-Asianism and the Legacy of the Chinese Revolution |page=25 |date=2023 |publisher=University of Chicago Press}}</ref>
==Influence== [[File:Mito rebellion samurai under Sonno Joi banner.jpg|thumb|Part of a woodblock print by [[:ja:歌川国輝 (3代目)|Utagawa Kuniteru III]] depicting [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] under a ''sonnō jōi'' banner during the 1864 [[:en:Mito Rebellion|Mito Rebellion]] ]] With the increasing number of incursions of foreign ships into Japanese waters in the late 18th and early 19th century, the ''[[:en:Sakoku|Sakoku]]'' ("national seclusion") policy came increasingly into question. The ''jōi'' "expel the barbarians" portion of ''sonnō jōi'', changed into a reaction against the [[:en:Convention of Kanagawa|Convention of Kanagawa]] of 1854, which opened Japan to foreign trade. Under military threat from [[:en:United States Navy|United States Navy]] [[Commodore (Rank)|Commodore]] [[:en:Matthew C. Perry|Matthew C. Perry]]'s so-called "[[:en:Black Ships|black ships]]", the treaty was signed under duress and was vehemently opposed in ''[[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]'' quarters. The fact that the Tokugawa Shogunate was powerless against the foreigners despite the will expressed by the Imperial court was taken as evidence by [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] and other anti-Tokugawa leaders that the ''sonnō'' (revere the Emperor) portion of the philosophy was not working, and that the Shogunate must be replaced by a government more able to show its loyalty to the Emperor by enforcing the Emperor’s will.
The philosophy was thus adopted as a battle cry of the rebellious regions of [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū Domain]] and [[:en:Satsuma Province|Satsuma Province]]. The Imperial court in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] sympathized with the movement. [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Emperor Kōmei]] personally agreed with such sentiments, and – breaking with centuries of imperial tradition – personally began to take an active role in matters of state: as opportunities arose, he fulminated against the treaties and attempted to interfere in the shogunal succession. His efforts culminated in March 1863 with his "[[:en:Order to Expel Barbarians|Order to Expel Barbarians]]" ({{lang|ja|攘夷勅命}}). Although the Shogunate had no intention of enforcing the order, it nevertheless inspired attacks against the Shogunate itself and against foreigners in Japan, the most notable incident being the killing of the trader [[:en:Charles Lennox Richardson|Charles Lennox Richardson]]. Other attacks included the shelling of foreign shipping in [[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki]].<ref>Hagiwara, p. 35.</ref> ''[[:en:Rōnin|Rōnin]]s'' (masterless samurai) also rallied to the cause, assassinating Shogunate officials and Westerners.
This turned out to be the zenith of the ''sonnō jōi'' movement, since the [[:en:Western world|Western powers]] responded by demanding [[:en:Reparations (transitional justice)|reparations]] for the assassinations and other acts by samurai against Western interests. In 1864, four Western nations launched a campaign against Shimonoseki, overrunning the meager defences and briefly occupying the region. While this incident showed that Japan was no match for Western military powers, it also served to further weaken the Shogunate, permitting the rebel provinces to ally and overthrow it, bringing about the [[Meiji Restoration]].
The slogan itself was never actually a government or rebel policy; for all its rhetoric, Satsuma in particular had close ties with the West, purchasing guns, artillery, ships and other technology.
==Legacy== After the symbolic restoration of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]], the ''sonnō jōi'' slogan was replaced with ''[[:en:Fukoku kyōhei|Fukoku kyōhei]]'' ([[:ja:富国強兵|富国強兵]]), or "enrich the nation, strengthen the armies", the rallying call of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and the seed of its actions during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].{{fact|date=November 2023}}
==See also== *This phrase is featured and examined in [[:en:James Clavell|James Clavell]]'s ''[[:en:Gai-Jin (novel)|Gai-Jin: A Novel of Japan]]'' *[[:en:Kinmon incident|Kinmon incident]] *''[[:en:Shishi (organization)|Shishi]]'' *[[:en:Shōwa Restoration|Shōwa Restoration]]

==References== * Akamatsu, Paul. (1972). ''Meiji 1868: Revolution and Counter-Revolution in Japan'' (Miriam Kochan, translator). New York: Harper & Row. * [[:en:William G. Beasley|Beasley, William G.]] (1972). ''The Meiji Restoration.'' Stanford: Stanford University Press. * [[:en:Albert M. Craig|Craig, Albert M.]] (1961). ''Chōshū in the Meiji Restoration.'' Cambridge: Harvard University Press. * [[:en:Marius Jansen|Jansen, Marius B.]] and [[:en:Gilbert Rozman|Gilbert Rozman]], eds. (1986). ''Japan in Transition: from Tokugawa to Meiji.'' Princeton: [[:en:Princeton University Press|Princeton University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780691054599}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/12311985 OCLC 12311985] * Jansen, Marius B. (2000). ''The Making of Modern Japan.'' Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780674003347}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/44090600 OCLC 44090600] *Shiba, Ryotaro. (1998). ''The Last Shogun: The Life of Tokugawa Yoshinobu''. Tokyo: Kodansha. {{ISBN|1-56836-246-3}}

Based on [[:en:Neo-Confucianism|Neo-Confucianism]] and [[:en:Kokugaku|Japanese nativism]], the movement sought to overthrow the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and restore the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. {{Infobox Chinese | t = 尊王攘夷 | kanji = 尊王攘夷 | kana = そんのうじょうい | romaji = Sonnō jōi | p = Zunwang Rangyi | j = zyun1 wong4 joeng4 ji4 }}
== Etymology == ''Sonnō jōi'' is the [[:en:On'yomi|Japanese reading]] of the Chinese idiom ''Zunwang Rangyi'' ({{lang|zh-Hant|尊王攘夷}}; {{lit}} "Revere the King, Expel the Barbarians"). During the [[:en:Spring and Autumn period|Spring and Autumn period]] of China, Chancellor [[:en:Guan Zhong|Guan Zhong]] of [[:en:Qi (state)|Qi]] initiated a policy known as ''Zunwang Rangyi'', in reference to the [[:en:Zhou kings|Zhou kings]].<ref name=po5-65>{{cite book | last=Poo | first=Mu-chou | title=Enemies of Civilization | year=2005 | publisher=State University of New York Press | location=Albany | isbn=0-7914-6364-8 | page=65}}</ref> Adopting and adhering to it, [[:en:Duke Huan of Qi|Duke Huan of Qi]] assembled the Chinese feudal lords to strike down the threat of barbarians from China.<ref>{{cite book | last=Tillman | first=Hoyt Cleveland | title=Utilitarian Confucianism: Ch'en Liang's Challenge to Chu Hsi | year=1982 | publisher=Harvard University Press | location=Cambridge | isbn=0-674-93176-9 | page=64}}</ref> For it, [[:en:Confucius|Confucius]] himself praised Guan Zhong for the preservation of Chinese civilization through the example of the [[:en:Hua-Yi distinction|contrast]] in the hairstyles and [[:en:Hanfu|clothing styles]] between them and barbaric peoples.<ref name=po5-65/> Through the ''[[:en:Analects|Analects]]'' of Confucius, the Chinese expression came to be transmitted to Japan as ''sonnō jōi''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Holcombe | first=Charles | title=A History of East Asia: From the Origins of Civilization to the Twenty-first Century | year=2010 | publisher=Cambridge University Press | location=Cambridge | isbn=978-0-521-73164-5 | page=216}}</ref>
== Philosophy == The origin of the philosophy as used in Japan can be traced to the Confucian classic the Gongyang Commentary of the ''Chunqiu''. The Tokugawa shogunate promulgated the Zhu Xi school of Neo-Confucianism (''Shushi-gaku''), which interpreted the ''Chunqiu'' using this concept. 17th-century Confucian scholars [[:en:Yamazaki Ansai|Yamazaki Ansai]] and [[:en:Yamaga Sokō|Yamaga Sokō]] wrote on the sanctity of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] and its superiority to the ruling houses of other nations. These ideas were expanded by [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] scholar [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], and seen in Takenouchi Shikibu's theory of absolute loyalty to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] ([[:ja:尊皇論|尊皇論]] ''sonnōron''), that implied that less loyalty should be given to the ruling Tokugawa shogunate.
[[:en:Mitogaku|Mitogaku]] scholar [[:en:Aizawa Seishisai|Aizawa Seishisai]] introduced the term ''sonnō jōi'' into modern Japanese in his work ''Shinron'' in 1825, where ''sonnō'' was regarded as the reverence expressed by the Tokugawa Shogunate to the emperor and ''jōi'' was the [[History of Roman Catholicism in Japan#Persecution and martyrdom|proscription of Christianity]].
''Sonnō jōi'' was an [[:en:anti-imperialist|anti-imperialist]] expression of [[:en:Western imperialism|Western imperialism]] before Japan transitioned to imperialism.<ref>{{cite book |editor=Viren Murthy |title=Pan-Asianism and the Legacy of the Chinese Revolution |page=25 |date=2023 |publisher=University of Chicago Press}}</ref>
==Influence== [[File:Mito rebellion samurai under Sonno Joi banner.jpg|thumb|Part of a woodblock print by [[:ja:歌川国輝 (3代目)|Utagawa Kuniteru III]] depicting [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] under a ''sonnō jōi'' banner during the 1864 [[:en:Mito Rebellion|Mito Rebellion]] ]] With the increasing number of incursions of foreign ships into Japanese waters in the late 18th and early 19th century, the ''[[:en:Sakoku|Sakoku]]'' ("national seclusion") policy came increasingly into question. The ''jōi'' "expel the barbarians" portion of ''sonnō jōi'', changed into a reaction against the [[:en:Convention of Kanagawa|Convention of Kanagawa]] of 1854, which opened Japan to foreign trade. Under military threat from [[:en:United States Navy|United States Navy]] [[Commodore (Rank)|Commodore]] [[:en:Matthew C. Perry|Matthew C. Perry]]'s so-called "[[:en:Black Ships|black ships]]", the treaty was signed under duress and was vehemently opposed in ''[[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]'' quarters. The fact that the Tokugawa Shogunate was powerless against the foreigners despite the will expressed by the Imperial court was taken as evidence by [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] and other anti-Tokugawa leaders that the ''sonnō'' (revere the Emperor) portion of the philosophy was not working, and that the Shogunate must be replaced by a government more able to show its loyalty to the Emperor by enforcing the Emperor’s will.
The philosophy was thus adopted as a battle cry of the rebellious regions of [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū Domain]] and [[:en:Satsuma Province|Satsuma Province]]. The Imperial court in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] sympathized with the movement. [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Emperor Kōmei]] personally agreed with such sentiments, and – breaking with centuries of imperial tradition – personally began to take an active role in matters of state: as opportunities arose, he fulminated against the treaties and attempted to interfere in the shogunal succession. His efforts culminated in March 1863 with his "[[:en:Order to Expel Barbarians|Order to Expel Barbarians]]" ({{lang|ja|攘夷勅命}}). Although the Shogunate had no intention of enforcing the order, it nevertheless inspired attacks against the Shogunate itself and against foreigners in Japan, the most notable incident being the killing of the trader [[:en:Charles Lennox Richardson|Charles Lennox Richardson]]. Other attacks included the shelling of foreign shipping in [[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki]].<ref>Hagiwara, p. 35.</ref> ''[[:en:Rōnin|Rōnin]]s'' (masterless samurai) also rallied to the cause, assassinating Shogunate officials and Westerners.
This turned out to be the zenith of the ''sonnō jōi'' movement, since the [[:en:Western world|Western powers]] responded by demanding [[:en:Reparations (transitional justice)|reparations]] for the assassinations and other acts by samurai against Western interests. In 1864, four Western nations launched a campaign against Shimonoseki, overrunning the meager defences and briefly occupying the region. While this incident showed that Japan was no match for Western military powers, it also served to further weaken the Shogunate, permitting the rebel provinces to ally and overthrow it, bringing about the [[Meiji Restoration]].
The slogan itself was never actually a government or rebel policy; for all its rhetoric, Satsuma in particular had close ties with the West, purchasing guns, artillery, ships and other technology.
==Legacy== After the symbolic restoration of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]], the ''sonnō jōi'' slogan was replaced with ''[[:en:Fukoku kyōhei|Fukoku kyōhei]]'' ([[:ja:富国強兵|富国強兵]]), or "enrich the nation, strengthen the armies", the rallying call of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and the seed of its actions during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].{{fact|date=November 2023}}
==See also== *This phrase is featured and examined in [[:en:James Clavell|James Clavell]]'s ''[[:en:Gai-Jin (novel)|Gai-Jin: A Novel of Japan]]'' *[[:en:Kinmon incident|Kinmon incident]] *''[[:en:Shishi (organization)|Shishi]]'' *[[:en:Shōwa Restoration|Shōwa Restoration]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Akamatsu, Paul. (1972). ''Meiji 1868: Revolution and Counter-Revolution in Japan'' (Miriam Kochan, translator). New York: Harper & Row. * [[:en:William G. Beasley|Beasley, William G.]] (1972). ''The Meiji Restoration.'' Stanford: Stanford University Press. * [[:en:Albert M. Craig|Craig, Albert M.]] (1961). ''Chōshū in the Meiji Restoration.'' Cambridge: Harvard University Press. * [[:en:Marius Jansen|Jansen, Marius B.]] and [[:en:Gilbert Rozman|Gilbert Rozman]], eds. (1986). ''Japan in Transition: from Tokugawa to Meiji.'' Princeton: [[:en:Princeton University Press|Princeton University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780691054599}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/12311985 OCLC 12311985] * Jansen, Marius B. (2000). ''The Making of Modern Japan.'' Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780674003347}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/44090600 OCLC 44090600] *Shiba, Ryotaro. (1998). ''The Last Shogun: The Life of Tokugawa Yoshinobu''. Tokyo: Kodansha. {{ISBN|1-56836-246-3}}

June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Nationalist slogan in 1850s Japan}}{{refimprove|date=September 2013}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Yokohama-Sumo-Wrestler-Defeating-a-Foreigner-1861-Ipposai-Yoshifuji.png|thumb|An 1861 image expressing the ''Jōi'' ({{lang|ja|攘夷}}, "Expel the Barbarians") sentiment]] {{nihongo|2=[[:wikt:尊|尊]][[:wikt:王|王]][[:wikt:攘|攘]][[:wikt:夷|夷]]|3='''Sonnō jōi'''|4="revere the Emperor, expel the barbarians"}} was a ''[[:en:Yojijukugo|Yojijukugo]]'' (four-character compound) phrase used as the rallying cry and [[:en:slogan|slogan]] of a [[:en:political movement|political movement]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] in the 1850s and 1860s, during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period. Based on [[:en:Neo-Confucianism|Neo-Confucianism]] and [[:en:Kokugaku|Japanese nativism]], the movement sought to overthrow the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and restore the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. {{Infobox Chinese | t = 尊王攘夷 | kanji = 尊王攘夷 | kana = そんのうじょうい | romaji = Sonnō jōi | p = Zunwang Rangyi | j = zyun1 wong4 joeng4 ji4 }}

Perry]]'s so-called "[[:en:Black Ships|black ships]]", the treaty was signed under duress and was vehemently opposed in ''[[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]'' quarters. di]][[zh-classical:尊王攘夷]][[zh-yue:尊王攘夷]][[zh:尊王攘夷]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1065356]] Wikidata name: Sonnō jōi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Nationalist slogan in 1850s Japan}}{{refimprove|date=September 2013}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Yokohama-Sumo-Wrestler-Defeating-a-Foreigner-1861-Ipposai-Yoshifuji.png|thumb|An 1861 image expressing the ''Jōi'' ({{lang|ja|攘夷}}, "Expel the Barbarians") sentiment]] {{nihongo|2=[[:wikt:尊|尊]][[:wikt:王|王]][[:wikt:攘|攘]][[:wikt:夷|夷]]|3='''Sonnō jōi'''|4="revere the Emperor, expel the barbarians"}} was a ''[[:en:Yojijukugo|Yojijukugo]]'' (four-character compound) phrase used as the rallying cry and [[:en:slogan|slogan]] of a [[:en:political movement|political movement]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] in the 1850s and 1860s, during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period. Based on [[:en:Neo-Confucianism|Neo-Confucianism]] and [[:en:Kokugaku|Japanese nativism]], the movement sought to overthrow the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and restore the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. {{Infobox Chinese | t = 尊王攘夷 | kanji = 尊王攘夷 | kana = そんのうじょうい | romaji = Sonnō jōi | p = Zunwang Rangyi | j = zyun1 wong4 joeng4 ji4 }}
== Etymology == ''Sonnō jōi'' is the [[:en:On'yomi|Japanese reading]] of the Chinese idiom ''Zunwang Rangyi'' ({{lang|zh-Hant|尊王攘夷}}; {{lit}} "Revere the King, Expel the Barbarians"). During the [[:en:Spring and Autumn period|Spring and Autumn period]] of China, Chancellor [[:en:Guan Zhong|Guan Zhong]] of [[:en:Qi (state)|Qi]] initiated a policy known as ''Zunwang Rangyi'', in reference to the [[:en:Zhou kings|Zhou kings]].<ref name=po5-65>{{cite book | last=Poo | first=Mu-chou | title=Enemies of Civilization | year=2005 | publisher=State University of New York Press | location=Albany | isbn=0-7914-6364-8 | page=65}}</ref> Adopting and adhering to it, [[:en:Duke Huan of Qi|Duke Huan of Qi]] assembled the Chinese feudal lords to strike down the threat of barbarians from China.<ref>{{cite book | last=Tillman | first=Hoyt Cleveland | title=Utilitarian Confucianism: Ch'en Liang's Challenge to Chu Hsi | year=1982 | publisher=Harvard University Press | location=Cambridge | isbn=0-674-93176-9 | page=64}}</ref> For it, [[:en:Confucius|Confucius]] himself praised Guan Zhong for the preservation of Chinese civilization through the example of the [[:en:Hua-Yi distinction|contrast]] in the hairstyles and [[:en:Hanfu|clothing styles]] between them and barbaric peoples.<ref name=po5-65/> Through the ''[[:en:Analects|Analects]]'' of Confucius, the Chinese expression came to be transmitted to Japan as ''sonnō jōi''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Holcombe | first=Charles | title=A History of East Asia: From the Origins of Civilization to the Twenty-first Century | year=2010 | publisher=Cambridge University Press | location=Cambridge | isbn=978-0-521-73164-5 | page=216}}</ref>
== Philosophy == The origin of the philosophy as used in Japan can be traced to the Confucian classic the Gongyang Commentary of the ''Chunqiu''. The Tokugawa shogunate promulgated the Zhu Xi school of Neo-Confucianism (''Shushi-gaku''), which interpreted the ''Chunqiu'' using this concept. 17th-century Confucian scholars [[:en:Yamazaki Ansai|Yamazaki Ansai]] and [[:en:Yamaga Sokō|Yamaga Sokō]] wrote on the sanctity of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] and its superiority to the ruling houses of other nations. These ideas were expanded by [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] scholar [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], and seen in Takenouchi Shikibu's theory of absolute loyalty to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] ([[:ja:尊皇論|尊皇論]] ''sonnōron''), that implied that less loyalty should be given to the ruling Tokugawa shogunate.
[[:en:Mitogaku|Mitogaku]] scholar [[:en:Aizawa Seishisai|Aizawa Seishisai]] introduced the term ''sonnō jōi'' into modern Japanese in his work ''Shinron'' in 1825, where ''sonnō'' was regarded as the reverence expressed by the Tokugawa Shogunate to the emperor and ''jōi'' was the [[History of Roman Catholicism in Japan#Persecution and martyrdom|proscription of Christianity]].
''Sonnō jōi'' was an [[:en:anti-imperialist|anti-imperialist]] expression of [[:en:Western imperialism|Western imperialism]] before Japan transitioned to imperialism.<ref>{{cite book |editor=Viren Murthy |title=Pan-Asianism and the Legacy of the Chinese Revolution |page=25 |date=2023 |publisher=University of Chicago Press}}</ref>
==Influence== [[File:Mito rebellion samurai under Sonno Joi banner.jpg|thumb|Part of a woodblock print by [[:ja:歌川国輝 (3代目)|Utagawa Kuniteru III]] depicting [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] under a ''sonnō jōi'' banner during the 1864 [[:en:Mito Rebellion|Mito Rebellion]] ]] With the increasing number of incursions of foreign ships into Japanese waters in the late 18th and early 19th century, the ''[[:en:Sakoku|Sakoku]]'' ("national seclusion") policy came increasingly into question. The ''jōi'' "expel the barbarians" portion of ''sonnō jōi'', changed into a reaction against the [[:en:Convention of Kanagawa|Convention of Kanagawa]] of 1854, which opened Japan to foreign trade. Under military threat from [[:en:United States Navy|United States Navy]] [[Commodore (Rank)|Commodore]] [[:en:Matthew C. Perry|Matthew C. Perry]]'s so-called "[[:en:Black Ships|black ships]]", the treaty was signed under duress and was vehemently opposed in ''[[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]'' quarters. The fact that the Tokugawa Shogunate was powerless against the foreigners despite the will expressed by the Imperial court was taken as evidence by [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] and other anti-Tokugawa leaders that the ''sonnō'' (revere the Emperor) portion of the philosophy was not working, and that the Shogunate must be replaced by a government more able to show its loyalty to the Emperor by enforcing the Emperor’s will.
The philosophy was thus adopted as a battle cry of the rebellious regions of [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū Domain]] and [[:en:Satsuma Province|Satsuma Province]]. The Imperial court in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] sympathized with the movement. [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Emperor Kōmei]] personally agreed with such sentiments, and – breaking with centuries of imperial tradition – personally began to take an active role in matters of state: as opportunities arose, he fulminated against the treaties and attempted to interfere in the shogunal succession. His efforts culminated in March 1863 with his "[[:en:Order to Expel Barbarians|Order to Expel Barbarians]]" ({{lang|ja|攘夷勅命}}). Although the Shogunate had no intention of enforcing the order, it nevertheless inspired attacks against the Shogunate itself and against foreigners in Japan, the most notable incident being the killing of the trader [[:en:Charles Lennox Richardson|Charles Lennox Richardson]]. Other attacks included the shelling of foreign shipping in [[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki]].<ref>Hagiwara, p. 35.</ref> ''[[:en:Rōnin|Rōnin]]s'' (masterless samurai) also rallied to the cause, assassinating Shogunate officials and Westerners.
This turned out to be the zenith of the ''sonnō jōi'' movement, since the [[:en:Western world|Western powers]] responded by demanding [[:en:Reparations (transitional justice)|reparations]] for the assassinations and other acts by samurai against Western interests. In 1864, four Western nations launched a campaign against Shimonoseki, overrunning the meager defences and briefly occupying the region. While this incident showed that Japan was no match for Western military powers, it also served to further weaken the Shogunate, permitting the rebel provinces to ally and overthrow it, bringing about the [[Meiji Restoration]].
The slogan itself was never actually a government or rebel policy; for all its rhetoric, Satsuma in particular had close ties with the West, purchasing guns, artillery, ships and other technology.
==Legacy== After the symbolic restoration of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]], the ''sonnō jōi'' slogan was replaced with ''[[:en:Fukoku kyōhei|Fukoku kyōhei]]'' ([[:ja:富国強兵|富国強兵]]), or "enrich the nation, strengthen the armies", the rallying call of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and the seed of its actions during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].{{fact|date=November 2023}}
==See also== *This phrase is featured and examined in [[:en:James Clavell|James Clavell]]'s ''[[:en:Gai-Jin (novel)|Gai-Jin: A Novel of Japan]]'' *[[:en:Kinmon incident|Kinmon incident]] *''[[:en:Shishi (organization)|Shishi]]'' *[[:en:Shōwa Restoration|Shōwa Restoration]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Akamatsu, Paul. (1972). ''Meiji 1868: Revolution and Counter-Revolution in Japan'' (Miriam Kochan, translator). New York: Harper & Row. * [[:en:William G. Beasley|Beasley, William G.]] (1972). ''The Meiji Restoration.'' Stanford: Stanford University Press. * [[:en:Albert M. Craig|Craig, Albert M.]] (1961). ''Chōshū in the Meiji Restoration.'' Cambridge: Harvard University Press. * [[:en:Marius Jansen|Jansen, Marius B.]] and [[:en:Gilbert Rozman|Gilbert Rozman]], eds. (1986). ''Japan in Transition: from Tokugawa to Meiji.'' Princeton: [[:en:Princeton University Press|Princeton University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780691054599}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/12311985 OCLC 12311985] * Jansen, Marius B. (2000). ''The Making of Modern Japan.'' Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780674003347}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/44090600 OCLC 44090600] *Shiba, Ryotaro. (1998). ''The Last Shogun: The Life of Tokugawa Yoshinobu''. Tokyo: Kodansha. {{ISBN|1-56836-246-3}}

Perry]]'s so-called "[[:en:Black Ships|black ships]]", the treaty was signed under duress and was vehemently opposed in ''[[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]'' quarters. This turned out to be the zenith of the ''sonnō jōi'' movement, since the [[:en:Western world|Western powers]] responded by demanding [[:en:Reparations (transitional justice)|reparations]] for the assassinations and other acts by samurai against Western interests. In 1864, four Western nations launched a campaign against Shimonoseki, overrunning the meager defences and briefly occupying the region. While this incident showed that Japan was no match for Western military powers, it also served to further weaken the Shogunate, permitting the rebel provinces to ally and overthrow it, bringing about the [[Meiji Restoration]].
The slogan itself was never actually a government or rebel policy; for all its rhetoric, Satsuma in particular had close ties with the West, purchasing guns, artillery, ships and other technology.
==Legacy== After the symbolic restoration of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]], the ''sonnō jōi'' slogan was replaced with ''[[:en:Fukoku kyōhei|Fukoku kyōhei]]'' ([[:ja:富国強兵|富国強兵]]), or "enrich the nation, strengthen the armies", the rallying call of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and the seed of its actions during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].{{fact|date=November 2023}}
==See also== *This phrase is featured and examined in [[:en:James Clavell|James Clavell]]'s ''[[:en:Gai-Jin (novel)|Gai-Jin: A Novel of Japan]]'' *[[:en:Kinmon incident|Kinmon incident]] *''[[:en:Shishi (organization)|Shishi]]'' *[[:en:Shōwa Restoration|Shōwa Restoration]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}

Beasley|Beasley, William G.]] (1972). ''The Meiji Restoration.'' Stanford: Stanford University Press. * [[:en:Albert M. Craig|Craig, Albert M.]] (1961). ''Chōshū in the Meiji Restoration.'' Cambridge: Harvard University Press. * [[:en:Marius Jansen|Jansen, Marius B.]] and [[:en:Gilbert Rozman|Gilbert Rozman]], eds. (1986). ''Japan in Transition: from Tokugawa to Meiji.'' Princeton: [[:en:Princeton University Press|Princeton University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780691054599}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/12311985 OCLC 12311985] * Jansen, Marius B. (2000). ''The Making of Modern Japan.'' Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780674003347}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/44090600 OCLC 44090600] *Shiba, Ryotaro. di]][[zh-classical:尊王攘夷]][[zh-yue:尊王攘夷]][[zh:尊王攘夷]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1065356]] Wikidata name: Sonnō jōi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Nationalist slogan in 1850s Japan}}{{refimprove|date=September 2013}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Yokohama-Sumo-Wrestler-Defeating-a-Foreigner-1861-Ipposai-Yoshifuji.png|thumb|An 1861 image expressing the ''Jōi'' ({{lang|ja|攘夷}}, "Expel the Barbarians") sentiment]] {{nihongo|2=[[:wikt:尊|尊]][[:wikt:王|王]][[:wikt:攘|攘]][[:wikt:夷|夷]]|3='''Sonnō jōi'''|4="revere the Emperor, expel the barbarians"}} was a ''[[:en:Yojijukugo|Yojijukugo]]'' (four-character compound) phrase used as the rallying cry and [[:en:slogan|slogan]] of a [[:en:political movement|political movement]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] in the 1850s and 1860s, during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period. Based on [[:en:Neo-Confucianism|Neo-Confucianism]] and [[:en:Kokugaku|Japanese nativism]], the movement sought to overthrow the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and restore the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. {{Infobox Chinese | t = 尊王攘夷 | kanji = 尊王攘夷 | kana = そんのうじょうい | romaji = Sonnō jōi | p = Zunwang Rangyi | j = zyun1 wong4 joeng4 ji4 }}
== Etymology == ''Sonnō jōi'' is the [[:en:On'yomi|Japanese reading]] of the Chinese idiom ''Zunwang Rangyi'' ({{lang|zh-Hant|尊王攘夷}}; {{lit}} "Revere the King, Expel the Barbarians"). During the [[:en:Spring and Autumn period|Spring and Autumn period]] of China, Chancellor [[:en:Guan Zhong|Guan Zhong]] of [[:en:Qi (state)|Qi]] initiated a policy known as ''Zunwang Rangyi'', in reference to the [[:en:Zhou kings|Zhou kings]].<ref name=po5-65>{{cite book | last=Poo | first=Mu-chou | title=Enemies of Civilization | year=2005 | publisher=State University of New York Press | location=Albany | isbn=0-7914-6364-8 | page=65}}</ref> Adopting and adhering to it, [[:en:Duke Huan of Qi|Duke Huan of Qi]] assembled the Chinese feudal lords to strike down the threat of barbarians from China.<ref>{{cite book | last=Tillman | first=Hoyt Cleveland | title=Utilitarian Confucianism: Ch'en Liang's Challenge to Chu Hsi | year=1982 | publisher=Harvard University Press | location=Cambridge | isbn=0-674-93176-9 | page=64}}</ref> For it, [[:en:Confucius|Confucius]] himself praised Guan Zhong for the preservation of Chinese civilization through the example of the [[:en:Hua-Yi distinction|contrast]] in the hairstyles and [[:en:Hanfu|clothing styles]] between them and barbaric peoples.<ref name=po5-65/> Through the ''[[:en:Analects|Analects]]'' of Confucius, the Chinese expression came to be transmitted to Japan as ''sonnō jōi''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Holcombe | first=Charles | title=A History of East Asia: From the Origins of Civilization to the Twenty-first Century | year=2010 | publisher=Cambridge University Press | location=Cambridge | isbn=978-0-521-73164-5 | page=216}}</ref>
== Philosophy == The origin of the philosophy as used in Japan can be traced to the Confucian classic the Gongyang Commentary of the ''Chunqiu''. The Tokugawa shogunate promulgated the Zhu Xi school of Neo-Confucianism (''Shushi-gaku''), which interpreted the ''Chunqiu'' using this concept. 17th-century Confucian scholars [[:en:Yamazaki Ansai|Yamazaki Ansai]] and [[:en:Yamaga Sokō|Yamaga Sokō]] wrote on the sanctity of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] and its superiority to the ruling houses of other nations. These ideas were expanded by [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] scholar [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], and seen in Takenouchi Shikibu's theory of absolute loyalty to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] ([[:ja:尊皇論|尊皇論]] ''sonnōron''), that implied that less loyalty should be given to the ruling Tokugawa shogunate.
[[:en:Mitogaku|Mitogaku]] scholar [[:en:Aizawa Seishisai|Aizawa Seishisai]] introduced the term ''sonnō jōi'' into modern Japanese in his work ''Shinron'' in 1825, where ''sonnō'' was regarded as the reverence expressed by the Tokugawa Shogunate to the emperor and ''jōi'' was the [[History of Roman Catholicism in Japan#Persecution and martyrdom|proscription of Christianity]].
''Sonnō jōi'' was an [[:en:anti-imperialist|anti-imperialist]] expression of [[:en:Western imperialism|Western imperialism]] before Japan transitioned to imperialism.<ref>{{cite book |editor=Viren Murthy |title=Pan-Asianism and the Legacy of the Chinese Revolution |page=25 |date=2023 |publisher=University of Chicago Press}}</ref>
==Influence== [[File:Mito rebellion samurai under Sonno Joi banner.jpg|thumb|Part of a woodblock print by [[:ja:歌川国輝 (3代目)|Utagawa Kuniteru III]] depicting [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] under a ''sonnō jōi'' banner during the 1864 [[:en:Mito Rebellion|Mito Rebellion]] ]] With the increasing number of incursions of foreign ships into Japanese waters in the late 18th and early 19th century, the ''[[:en:Sakoku|Sakoku]]'' ("national seclusion") policy came increasingly into question. The ''jōi'' "expel the barbarians" portion of ''sonnō jōi'', changed into a reaction against the [[:en:Convention of Kanagawa|Convention of Kanagawa]] of 1854, which opened Japan to foreign trade. Under military threat from [[:en:United States Navy|United States Navy]] [[Commodore (Rank)|Commodore]] [[:en:Matthew C. Perry|Matthew C. Perry]]'s so-called "[[:en:Black Ships|black ships]]", the treaty was signed under duress and was vehemently opposed in ''[[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]'' quarters. The fact that the Tokugawa Shogunate was powerless against the foreigners despite the will expressed by the Imperial court was taken as evidence by [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] and other anti-Tokugawa leaders that the ''sonnō'' (revere the Emperor) portion of the philosophy was not working, and that the Shogunate must be replaced by a government more able to show its loyalty to the Emperor by enforcing the Emperor’s will.
The philosophy was thus adopted as a battle cry of the rebellious regions of [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū Domain]] and [[:en:Satsuma Province|Satsuma Province]]. The Imperial court in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] sympathized with the movement. [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Emperor Kōmei]] personally agreed with such sentiments, and – breaking with centuries of imperial tradition – personally began to take an active role in matters of state: as opportunities arose, he fulminated against the treaties and attempted to interfere in the shogunal succession. His efforts culminated in March 1863 with his "[[:en:Order to Expel Barbarians|Order to Expel Barbarians]]" ({{lang|ja|攘夷勅命}}). Although the Shogunate had no intention of enforcing the order, it nevertheless inspired attacks against the Shogunate itself and against foreigners in Japan, the most notable incident being the killing of the trader [[:en:Charles Lennox Richardson|Charles Lennox Richardson]]. Other attacks included the shelling of foreign shipping in [[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki]].<ref>Hagiwara, p. 35.</ref> ''[[:en:Rōnin|Rōnin]]s'' (masterless samurai) also rallied to the cause, assassinating Shogunate officials and Westerners.
This turned out to be the zenith of the ''sonnō jōi'' movement, since the [[:en:Western world|Western powers]] responded by demanding [[:en:Reparations (transitional justice)|reparations]] for the assassinations and other acts by samurai against Western interests. In 1864, four Western nations launched a campaign against Shimonoseki, overrunning the meager defences and briefly occupying the region. While this incident showed that Japan was no match for Western military powers, it also served to further weaken the Shogunate, permitting the rebel provinces to ally and overthrow it, bringing about the [[Meiji Restoration]].
The slogan itself was never actually a government or rebel policy; for all its rhetoric, Satsuma in particular had close ties with the West, purchasing guns, artillery, ships and other technology.
==Legacy== After the symbolic restoration of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]], the ''sonnō jōi'' slogan was replaced with ''[[:en:Fukoku kyōhei|Fukoku kyōhei]]'' ([[:ja:富国強兵|富国強兵]]), or "enrich the nation, strengthen the armies", the rallying call of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and the seed of its actions during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].{{fact|date=November 2023}}
==See also== *This phrase is featured and examined in [[:en:James Clavell|James Clavell]]'s ''[[:en:Gai-Jin (novel)|Gai-Jin: A Novel of Japan]]'' *[[:en:Kinmon incident|Kinmon incident]] *''[[:en:Shishi (organization)|Shishi]]'' *[[:en:Shōwa Restoration|Shōwa Restoration]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Akamatsu, Paul. (1972). ''Meiji 1868: Revolution and Counter-Revolution in Japan'' (Miriam Kochan, translator). New York: Harper & Row. * [[:en:William G. Beasley|Beasley, William G.]] (1972). ''The Meiji Restoration.'' Stanford: Stanford University Press. * [[:en:Albert M. Craig|Craig, Albert M.]] (1961). ''Chōshū in the Meiji Restoration.'' Cambridge: Harvard University Press. * [[:en:Marius Jansen|Jansen, Marius B.]] and [[:en:Gilbert Rozman|Gilbert Rozman]], eds. (1986). ''Japan in Transition: from Tokugawa to Meiji.'' Princeton: [[:en:Princeton University Press|Princeton University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780691054599}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/12311985 OCLC 12311985] * Jansen, Marius B. (2000). ''The Making of Modern Japan.'' Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780674003347}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/44090600 OCLC 44090600] *Shiba, Ryotaro. (1998). ''The Last Shogun: The Life of Tokugawa Yoshinobu''. Tokyo: Kodansha. {{ISBN|1-56836-246-3}}

== Etymology == ''Sonnō jōi'' is the [[:en:On'yomi|Japanese reading]] of the Chinese idiom ''Zunwang Rangyi'' ({{lang|zh-Hant|尊王攘夷}}; {{lit}} "Revere the King, Expel the Barbarians"). During the [[:en:Spring and Autumn period|Spring and Autumn period]] of China, Chancellor [[:en:Guan Zhong|Guan Zhong]] of [[:en:Qi (state)|Qi]] initiated a policy known as ''Zunwang Rangyi'', in reference to the [[:en:Zhou kings|Zhou kings]].<ref name=po5-65>{{cite book | last=Poo | first=Mu-chou | title=Enemies of Civilization | year=2005 | publisher=State University of New York Press | location=Albany | isbn=0-7914-6364-8 | page=65}}</ref> Adopting and adhering to it, [[:en:Duke Huan of Qi|Duke Huan of Qi]] assembled the Chinese feudal lords to strike down the threat of barbarians from China.<ref>{{cite book | last=Tillman | first=Hoyt Cleveland | title=Utilitarian Confucianism: Ch'en Liang's Challenge to Chu Hsi | year=1982 | publisher=Harvard University Press | location=Cambridge | isbn=0-674-93176-9 | page=64}}</ref> For it, [[:en:Confucius|Confucius]] himself praised Guan Zhong for the preservation of Chinese civilization through the example of the [[:en:Hua-Yi distinction|contrast]] in the hairstyles and [[:en:Hanfu|clothing styles]] between them and barbaric peoples.<ref name=po5-65/> Through the ''[[:en:Analects|Analects]]'' of Confucius, the Chinese expression came to be transmitted to Japan as ''sonnō jōi''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Holcombe | first=Charles | title=A History of East Asia: From the Origins of Civilization to the Twenty-first Century | year=2010 | publisher=Cambridge University Press | location=Cambridge | isbn=978-0-521-73164-5 | page=216}}</ref>
== Philosophy == The origin of the philosophy as used in Japan can be traced to the Confucian classic the Gongyang Commentary of the ''Chunqiu''. The Tokugawa shogunate promulgated the Zhu Xi school of Neo-Confucianism (''Shushi-gaku''), which interpreted the ''Chunqiu'' using this concept. 17th-century Confucian scholars [[:en:Yamazaki Ansai|Yamazaki Ansai]] and [[:en:Yamaga Sokō|Yamaga Sokō]] wrote on the sanctity of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] and its superiority to the ruling houses of other nations. These ideas were expanded by [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] scholar [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], and seen in Takenouchi Shikibu's theory of absolute loyalty to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] ([[:ja:尊皇論|尊皇論]] ''sonnōron''), that implied that less loyalty should be given to the ruling Tokugawa shogunate.
[[:en:Mitogaku|Mitogaku]] scholar [[:en:Aizawa Seishisai|Aizawa Seishisai]] introduced the term ''sonnō jōi'' into modern Japanese in his work ''Shinron'' in 1825, where ''sonnō'' was regarded as the reverence expressed by the Tokugawa Shogunate to the emperor and ''jōi'' was the [[History of Roman Catholicism in Japan#Persecution and martyrdom|proscription of Christianity]].
''Sonnō jōi'' was an [[:en:anti-imperialist|anti-imperialist]] expression of [[:en:Western imperialism|Western imperialism]] before Japan transitioned to imperialism.<ref>{{cite book |editor=Viren Murthy |title=Pan-Asianism and the Legacy of the Chinese Revolution |page=25 |date=2023 |publisher=University of Chicago Press}}</ref>
==Influence== [[File:Mito rebellion samurai under Sonno Joi banner.jpg|thumb|Part of a woodblock print by [[:ja:歌川国輝 (3代目)|Utagawa Kuniteru III]] depicting [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] under a ''sonnō jōi'' banner during the 1864 [[:en:Mito Rebellion|Mito Rebellion]] ]] With the increasing number of incursions of foreign ships into Japanese waters in the late 18th and early 19th century, the ''[[:en:Sakoku|Sakoku]]'' ("national seclusion") policy came increasingly into question. The ''jōi'' "expel the barbarians" portion of ''sonnō jōi'', changed into a reaction against the [[:en:Convention of Kanagawa|Convention of Kanagawa]] of 1854, which opened Japan to foreign trade. Under military threat from [[:en:United States Navy|United States Navy]] [[Commodore (Rank)|Commodore]] [[:en:Matthew C. Perry|Matthew C. Perry]]'s so-called "[[:en:Black Ships|black ships]]", the treaty was signed under duress and was vehemently opposed in ''[[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]'' quarters. The fact that the Tokugawa Shogunate was powerless against the foreigners despite the will expressed by the Imperial court was taken as evidence by [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] and other anti-Tokugawa leaders that the ''sonnō'' (revere the Emperor) portion of the philosophy was not working, and that the Shogunate must be replaced by a government more able to show its loyalty to the Emperor by enforcing the Emperor’s will.
The philosophy was thus adopted as a battle cry of the rebellious regions of [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū Domain]] and [[:en:Satsuma Province|Satsuma Province]]. The Imperial court in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] sympathized with the movement. [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Emperor Kōmei]] personally agreed with such sentiments, and – breaking with centuries of imperial tradition – personally began to take an active role in matters of state: as opportunities arose, he fulminated against the treaties and attempted to interfere in the shogunal succession. His efforts culminated in March 1863 with his "[[:en:Order to Expel Barbarians|Order to Expel Barbarians]]" ({{lang|ja|攘夷勅命}}). Although the Shogunate had no intention of enforcing the order, it nevertheless inspired attacks against the Shogunate itself and against foreigners in Japan, the most notable incident being the killing of the trader [[:en:Charles Lennox Richardson|Charles Lennox Richardson]]. Other attacks included the shelling of foreign shipping in [[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki]].<ref>Hagiwara, p. 35.</ref> ''[[:en:Rōnin|Rōnin]]s'' (masterless samurai) also rallied to the cause, assassinating Shogunate officials and Westerners.
This turned out to be the zenith of the ''sonnō jōi'' movement, since the [[:en:Western world|Western powers]] responded by demanding [[:en:Reparations (transitional justice)|reparations]] for the assassinations and other acts by samurai against Western interests. In 1864, four Western nations launched a campaign against Shimonoseki, overrunning the meager defences and briefly occupying the region. While this incident showed that Japan was no match for Western military powers, it also served to further weaken the Shogunate, permitting the rebel provinces to ally and overthrow it, bringing about the [[Meiji Restoration]].
The slogan itself was never actually a government or rebel policy; for all its rhetoric, Satsuma in particular had close ties with the West, purchasing guns, artillery, ships and other technology.
==Legacy== After the symbolic restoration of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]], the ''sonnō jōi'' slogan was replaced with ''[[:en:Fukoku kyōhei|Fukoku kyōhei]]'' ([[:ja:富国強兵|富国強兵]]), or "enrich the nation, strengthen the armies", the rallying call of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and the seed of its actions during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].{{fact|date=November 2023}}
==See also== *This phrase is featured and examined in [[:en:James Clavell|James Clavell]]'s ''[[:en:Gai-Jin (novel)|Gai-Jin: A Novel of Japan]]'' *[[:en:Kinmon incident|Kinmon incident]] *''[[:en:Shishi (organization)|Shishi]]'' *[[:en:Shōwa Restoration|Shōwa Restoration]]

Beasley|Beasley, William G.]] (1972). ''The Meiji Restoration.'' Stanford: Stanford University Press. * [[:en:Albert M. Craig|Craig, Albert M.]] (1961). ''Chōshū in the Meiji Restoration.'' Cambridge: Harvard University Press. * [[:en:Marius Jansen|Jansen, Marius B.]] and [[:en:Gilbert Rozman|Gilbert Rozman]], eds. (1986). ''Japan in Transition: from Tokugawa to Meiji.'' Princeton: [[:en:Princeton University Press|Princeton University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780691054599}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/12311985 OCLC 12311985] * Jansen, Marius B. (2000). ''The Making of Modern Japan.'' Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780674003347}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/44090600 OCLC 44090600] *Shiba, Ryotaro. (1998). ''The Last Shogun: The Life of Tokugawa Yoshinobu''. Tokyo: Kodansha. {{ISBN|1-56836-246-3}}

June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Nationalist slogan in 1850s Japan}}{{refimprove|date=September 2013}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Yokohama-Sumo-Wrestler-Defeating-a-Foreigner-1861-Ipposai-Yoshifuji.png|thumb|An 1861 image expressing the ''Jōi'' ({{lang|ja|攘夷}}, "Expel the Barbarians") sentiment]] {{nihongo|2=[[:wikt:尊|尊]][[:wikt:王|王]][[:wikt:攘|攘]][[:wikt:夷|夷]]|3='''Sonnō jōi'''|4="revere the Emperor, expel the barbarians"}} was a ''[[:en:Yojijukugo|Yojijukugo]]'' (four-character compound) phrase used as the rallying cry and [[:en:slogan|slogan]] of a [[:en:political movement|political movement]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] in the 1850s and 1860s, during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period. Based on [[:en:Neo-Confucianism|Neo-Confucianism]] and [[:en:Kokugaku|Japanese nativism]], the movement sought to overthrow the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and restore the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. {{Infobox Chinese | t = 尊王攘夷 | kanji = 尊王攘夷 | kana = そんのうじょうい | romaji = Sonnō jōi | p = Zunwang Rangyi | j = zyun1 wong4 joeng4 ji4 }}
== Etymology == ''Sonnō jōi'' is the [[:en:On'yomi|Japanese reading]] of the Chinese idiom ''Zunwang Rangyi'' ({{lang|zh-Hant|尊王攘夷}}; {{lit}} "Revere the King, Expel the Barbarians"). During the [[:en:Spring and Autumn period|Spring and Autumn period]] of China, Chancellor [[:en:Guan Zhong|Guan Zhong]] of [[:en:Qi (state)|Qi]] initiated a policy known as ''Zunwang Rangyi'', in reference to the [[:en:Zhou kings|Zhou kings]].<ref name=po5-65>{{cite book | last=Poo | first=Mu-chou | title=Enemies of Civilization | year=2005 | publisher=State University of New York Press | location=Albany | isbn=0-7914-6364-8 | page=65}}</ref> Adopting and adhering to it, [[:en:Duke Huan of Qi|Duke Huan of Qi]] assembled the Chinese feudal lords to strike down the threat of barbarians from China.<ref>{{cite book | last=Tillman | first=Hoyt Cleveland | title=Utilitarian Confucianism: Ch'en Liang's Challenge to Chu Hsi | year=1982 | publisher=Harvard University Press | location=Cambridge | isbn=0-674-93176-9 | page=64}}</ref> For it, [[:en:Confucius|Confucius]] himself praised Guan Zhong for the preservation of Chinese civilization through the example of the [[:en:Hua-Yi distinction|contrast]] in the hairstyles and [[:en:Hanfu|clothing styles]] between them and barbaric peoples.<ref name=po5-65/> Through the ''[[:en:Analects|Analects]]'' of Confucius, the Chinese expression came to be transmitted to Japan as ''sonnō jōi''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Holcombe | first=Charles | title=A History of East Asia: From the Origins of Civilization to the Twenty-first Century | year=2010 | publisher=Cambridge University Press | location=Cambridge | isbn=978-0-521-73164-5 | page=216}}</ref>
== Philosophy == The origin of the philosophy as used in Japan can be traced to the Confucian classic the Gongyang Commentary of the ''Chunqiu''. The Tokugawa shogunate promulgated the Zhu Xi school of Neo-Confucianism (''Shushi-gaku''), which interpreted the ''Chunqiu'' using this concept. 17th-century Confucian scholars [[:en:Yamazaki Ansai|Yamazaki Ansai]] and [[:en:Yamaga Sokō|Yamaga Sokō]] wrote on the sanctity of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] and its superiority to the ruling houses of other nations. These ideas were expanded by [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] scholar [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], and seen in Takenouchi Shikibu's theory of absolute loyalty to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] ([[:ja:尊皇論|尊皇論]] ''sonnōron''), that implied that less loyalty should be given to the ruling Tokugawa shogunate.
[[:en:Mitogaku|Mitogaku]] scholar [[:en:Aizawa Seishisai|Aizawa Seishisai]] introduced the term ''sonnō jōi'' into modern Japanese in his work ''Shinron'' in 1825, where ''sonnō'' was regarded as the reverence expressed by the Tokugawa Shogunate to the emperor and ''jōi'' was the [[History of Roman Catholicism in Japan#Persecution and martyrdom|proscription of Christianity]].
